_comment: Scenario-staged routing-around-terrain demo: a brittle player cavalry unit (60 mounts at morale 3) stands just south of the impassable hill; an enemy cavalry charge from its WESTERN flank breaks it on contact. The flank angle is deliberate, learned by dumping three failed stagings: a charge from dead behind overshoots NORTH onto the flee lane and physically walls it (the mob gets knocked back south off its own escape route), a rear cavalry pursuer that stays in contact herds and grinds the mob before any detour shows, and archer fire pins the mob prone into a crawl. A flank charge overshoots EAST instead, so the northward flee lane stays open: the rout streams north, meets the hill dead ahead, and PathField.next_step_fleeing() bends it around the west corner with the unit's own clearance margin -- the same routing an ordered march gets -- while the recovering pursuer traces the same corridor behind it. This scenario does NOT demonstrate the separate 'stands and fights when genuinely trapped' behavior -- the hill is a single free-standing obstacle nowhere near a field edge, so terrain alone can never fully enclose a unit here; that behavior is covered by test_trapped_routing_unit_fights_instead_of_fleeing, which stages a real sealed ring directly. No player input needed; the scenario + seed make the clash deterministic. Sampled ticks pin the router's story beats (spawn, break, corner-rounding, the catch at the far shoulder): the pursuer's own formation progressively deforms during the long gallop chase -- a known, separately tracked sim/calibration question with fresh data posted from this retune -- so mid-gallop samples of the pursuer's cosmetics are deliberately not part of this clip's declared checks.
